speaker: interviewer
text: -22, 1995. We're in the home of court reporter-- Nuremberg court reporter Vivien Spitz in Aurora, Colorado, United States of America. My name is Gary Lubell, and we're conducting this interview in English. Now, Vivien, you came to Denver in, you said, 1952?

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry, '55.

speaker: interviewee
text: In August of '55.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what are some of the things you've done since then in your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 19-- my husband was on military duty here, so I went back into the court system. In order to do that, I had to pass the Colorado State statutory examination for a court reporter at 200 words a minute. And I received an officialship in 1956 in the Denver Superior Court, which is an inferior court to the Denver District Court-- in other states, the equivalent of superior courts, as in New York. And I worked in the court system and went to the Denver District Court in 1960, until 1972. At that time-- in 1969-- 1968, I had passed a National Court Reporter's Association examination, high-speed shorthand at 260 words a minute. And there were only four of us in the United States writing manual, or pen, shorthand who had passed this examination. So for that reason, I came to the attention of the United States Senate reporters. They needed to start training a new reporter-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] a new reporter, and [LAUGHS] they contacted me. I was teaching seminars, reporting seminars, all over the country at that time, partially for the National Court Reporters Association at conventions. I was at a convention in Boston in-- in 1967, I think it was. I know it was '69-- 1969. And through one of their reporters, whom I did not know, I received an invitation asking me if I would be interested in being the first woman reporter in the United States Senate. And to leave my Denver court and go back there to start training. I had never been to Washington, DC, and I didn't know anything about Washington. My two sons here and I were skiers and did not really want to leave the mountains in--. So I thanked them for the great honor but declined, with a big pay increase from my Denver court. Then in-- that was-- that was really 1968. In 1969, I was teaching. That was Milwaukee, '68, '69. I was teaching a seminar in Boston. And the-- the Secretary of the Senate, who was a very influential man, very important man in the Senate, learned that I was teaching up there, and he telephoned me and asked me if I wouldn't please, when I finished there, go down to Washington and take a look at them and the capital, since I've never been to Washington.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my younger son, Peter, was with me at that time, and we decided we would do that. Went down to Washington and to the capital and saw a beautiful city, and saw a capital that I was just in awe of. I was so impressed.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so then I told them that yes, I would. I would. But there was no opening at that time. But there-- in the future, there would be at some point. And it was a matter of training one from being a court reporter to becoming a parliamentary reporter with all the proper parliamentary procedure under the Senate rules. And I-- I went, eventually was called back there out of my Denver court for weeks and months at a time, just like it's going on in Washington, DC right now. 30- and 24-hour days-- I mean, 30-- 33 hours nonstop over days, and-- which they're doing. And then the first appointment came permanent in the House of Representatives in 1972. So I-- I was-- the Senate reporters knew I would take the first permanent appointment wherever it happened. I know that they wanted me to-- wanted it to be in the Senate, but nobody was retiring. The last man who retired there was 84.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was working in my Denver court down here, the District Court, with Judge Robert T. Kingsley, when the call came at the end of May, 1972 from the Speaker's office to my court. Speaker Carl Albert of Oklahoma, whom I did not know, and asking to speak to me. So my judge immediately-- he knew what was in the works eventually-- called a recess. And I went, and he asked me if I could be in Washington, DC the next day to be sworn in as a reporter of debates. And then I could return to Denver and finish a period of two weeks' notice, which is what I did.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then it was in '72 that I-- in June 19th, right around Watergate time, that I went to Washington, DC to report as an official reporter of debates on the team there in the United States House of Representatives.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, subsequent to that, I understand that you've received quite a few awards. But there was one very special one that took place in Congress, isn't that correct?

speaker: interviewee
text: While I was-- I did not receive an award in Congress, Gary, but I was a court reporter-- and I think this is very important-- in 1978. I mean, I was a reporter of debates in 1978, when President Jimmy Carter established the President's Commission on the Holocaust, appointing Elie Wiesel as chairman. And I was invited to the special ceremony in the rotunda, because I was the only court-- court reporter at Nuremberg who was part of the Congress, to this wonderful, special ceremony with Elie Wiesel, telling his story with his eight-year-old boy there then. And that was a great honor for me to be able to go to that. Then in 1979, President Carter established, by executive order, days of remembrance of victims of the Holocaust for April 28 and 29 1979 at-- in commemoration of-- asking the people of the United States quote, 'to observe this solemn anniversary in commemoration of the liberation of Dachau by US armed forces in-- on April 28, 29 1945.' That was--. And from that time forward, I really felt that something was going to really start occurring as far as education on the Holocaust that had not been going on, other than in the Jewish World. And I could never understand why.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was in 19-- [LAUGHS] when I left Nuremberg and went back to Detroit, I had nightmares for three years. I was always escaping in a tunnel with little children. It must have been tunnels under the barbed wire fences at the camp with the Nazi guards walking back and forth with their bayoneted weapons and trying to keep little children from crying or making any noise in this escape with other people. It was the same recurring nightmare for three years. Nothing different, ever. So in 1978 while I was in Congress, when the miniseries came on television four nights, there were four congressman, two Jewish and two non-Jewish, who asked me to watch it because they wanted to discuss it with me, knowing I had been at Nuremberg. And I watched that first night with the firebombing of the synagogue.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I had the same recurring nightmare, which was 30 years later. So I could not-- I told them I couldn't watch the other three nights. Now, today, I can watch anything, of course. But I was really very surprised that 30 years later I could not.

speaker: interviewer
text: At what point did you decide to start speaking out, and why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I came-- I came home by my terms. I-- when I went to the House, I made a commitment to myself that I would not stay more than 10 years, whereas others stay to the end of their lives sometimes. But I had discussed the stress of the work, and I was a-- a chief reporter over 19 people-- seven other reporters of debates, eight transcribers, and four clerks. And it was a very stressful job, especially when we worked 12 hours a day. And so I came back in 1982. And in 1983, my judge whom I worked for here in Denver when I went to Congress, Judge Robert T. Kingsley, who is deceased, asked me if I would go back with him in the Denver court system for the two years until he retired in 1985. And I did. But it was-- whenever anyone knew that I worked on the Nuremberg war trials, they would ask me if I would speak about it. And I did, in a very relaxed kind of setting, just storytelling what I remembered. And from 1985 on, I became very aware then of all the Holocaust denial. It was 1989, 1988 where we had a teacher right here at Hinkley High School in Aurora denying that the Holocaust ever occurred. And then I decided that I thought that it was time for me to start putting together a serious presentation. I had all of these files I brought with me. I was, you know, loading myself with information. And I had all these pictures that I brought from Nuremberg, Associated Press black-and-white glossies. And I had acquired others from people who knew I reported the trials. So I really started putting it together-- 10 years now.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it's been 10 years, and approximately how many people do you think you've spoken to and what sorts of groups?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have spoken-- the first group in Denver was a Catholic grade school, Saint Theresa's in Aurora, Colorado. These kids had as an assignment the reading of And I went out there that day to a Catholic grade school, and it was one of the most rewarding I've ever given. And those kids kept me all during their lunch hour with the nuns and the other teachers, asking questions. I have spoken over these years to middle schools, high schools, colleges, universities, professional organizations-- the National Court Reporters Association had me do their showcase in 1991 at the New York Hilton to over 1,000 people, including New York judges-- and bar associations, a lot of service organizations-- Lions, Kiwanis, Optimist, and I'm a member of the Optimist Club out here, such organizations, and reporters-- regional conventions, and the United States Air Force Academy, which to me, was a very important one on three occasions, now. In 1994-- especially have I been doing-- asked to do presentations during Holocaust Awareness Week. And I learned in Colorado that-- I think Governor Lamm began that in 1983 after my experience with Carter in Congress setting this up. So 1994, I received an invitation to do the very first Holocaust Awareness Week program at the United States Air Force Academy to-- first to the 89 officers, to the 89 officers. And they-- they were all men, with the exception of about four women, colonels, officers. And when I finished that day with my slides and the presentation that I give, there was an instant standing ovation, which really moved me, these, you know, people. And then they called me immediately to present to the cadets. And that-- the first speech was in April. And I spoke to the cadets down there on May 10th. And it was on a Friday night, the night that the cadets have off to go into Colorado Springs, a free night, or they could attend any one of 15 seminars they had presented that-- to present that evening. And mine was-- and get credit for it-- and mine was number eight on the list, with my title, 'Reporting the Nazi Doctors Case of the Nuremberg War Crimes Trials.' Of 900 cadets third-class, who are the sophomores, 368 selected my talk. And they had to move me from a classroom that was smaller into the one that would accommodate 400 people. And they showed the slides from their projector box onto a ceiling-to-floor stage screen. And there were cadets there who came up to me afterwards and said they never knew anything about the Holocaust, and they were interested. And then I had another call to go down and present to the cadets again a year ago November 19th, but I had already made a commitment and was in Phoenix giving a presentation down there.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's been 50 years since the Nuremberg trials. What do you think we've learned?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think that we have learned that number one, that we have to educate about it. We have to educate students. And I now believe-- I present to a lot of seventh and eighth grade students, who are the best. And I-- as long as they have had some kind of education, whether it's or other material that has been developed of an educational nature, highly developed by the University of Denver in bibliographies, that we are going to have much more of an understanding among young people-- and I think it should be earlier than seventh and eighth grade-- about the differences among people and the ethnic, racial, and religious differences, and that we have to respect-- respect is the word that I want to use-- the differences in people, the--.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I think that this is so important, and to respect their differences, to educate about the Holocaust, about what happened at that time. And there is education going on in, certainly, the Denver school system as a result of the University of Denver bringing the Anne Frank exhibit here in 1990, was it? '91. I can't remember, but I worked on that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- the-- we have to learn-- and-- and I always stress this-- that we've got to get rid of the hatred and the prejudice that we have, whether it's against the blacks, the Jews, the Muslims, the Protestants, the Catholics-- always from on the other side of a spectrum.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you look back on your experience and the fact that you chose to go to Nuremberg and report on the-- on the trials, if you were 21 years old today and you had that opportunity, what would you choose to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I would do the same, to go to Germany to work on the trials. Oh, absolutely.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because of the experience and learning what went on-- learning how, why-- trying to learn why. And I never could understand why, how a country like Germany, a modern, civilized society of my heritage-- I'm not Jewish, I'm half-German-- could look the other way to the extent that those who knew did. And a society that produced such brilliant writers, composers, and scientists-- Goethe, Kant, Bach, Beethoven, and yes, Hitler's Wagner, Bessel, Roentgen, and Einstein. I feel very fortunate to have been able to somehow, some way, been so driven to go there to work as a reporter. And my mother was so devastated, you know, to have me go.

speaker: interviewer
text: One final question, and that is for future generations, what message do you have based on your experience and what you've been through throughout your life in this experience in Nuremberg? What would you say to future generations about what you've learned?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have learned what happens with the word 'genocide.' And I think that in our high schools, we should pay more attention to education about the past and about the genocide. This-- this century has been the most violent century, beginning with the Turkish genocide of the Armenians in 1915 to 1919, all through these years-- Cambodia, Rwanda, Somalia, the Ukraine. And I-- I would just stress, I have to say, the word 'education,' again. Become educated. I feel--. And-- and to-- to other people who have an opportunity to be fortunate enough to-- to go to a foreign country, where you really can get into the culture of other people around the world, wherever it is.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any-- any closing thoughts?

speaker: interviewee
text: I always quote [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the philosopher, Spanish-born, Harvard-educated George Santayana, who wrote in 1905, 'those who cannot remember the past are condemned to repeat it.' And that we just have to remember the history of the past so that hopefully, we will not repeat it. Or if it is repeated, that we will not turn our heads and look the other way, as so many foreign heads of state, church leaders, and others did during this genocide. And we have to overcome these prejudices to structure our future with faith, hope, and international understanding, [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] peace, and justice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Vivien, I must say as your interviewer today that this has been a great honor for me to spend this time with you and to listen to your testimony and to record your testimony. I thank you very much. You're an inspiration, a courageous woman, and a very lovely person. And I thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you, Gary. I appreciate your having been my interviewer, and I'm highly honored to even sit here in this position to tell my story from a different perspective, maybe even that of a survivor.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.
